segments.title: Regional Market Area Mobility Improvements — $3.5M Pedestrian/Bicycle Project Overview
segments.summary: Public Transportation Committee chair Marino L. Nave convenes meeting on the RTC/Regional Market area mobility improvement project, a $3.5M federally and state-funded pedestrian and bicycle infrastructure project. City engineer Marza Malach presents: project is in preliminary design, covering Park Street from Hiawatha Boulevard to NBT Parkway and connecting streets. Improvements include 8-10 foot shared-use paths, new ADA-compliant crosswalks with push buttons, and mid-block crossings near the Regional Transportation Center. Construction estimated spring 2027, potentially delayed by NYS DOT review backlogs. Councilor Nave frames the project as a 20-year advocacy effort for north side residents without vehicles, particularly new Americans who walk to the regional market.

political_signal.description: Councilor Nave has championed north side pedestrian infrastructure for 20 years, framing this as essential for new Americans and residents without vehicles

speakers.name: Marino L. Nave
speakers.role: Committee Chair (Public Transportation), Common Councilor District 1

processing_notes: detected_amounts '$3' is truncated — actual amount is $3.5 million in federal/state funding
Public comment deadline mentioned: Friday March 21, 2026
Project connects to Washington Square shared-use path installed by DPW previous year

detected_departments: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs
segments.title: Shared-Use Path Design Details — Crosswalks, Safety Beacons, and Streetscape Integration
segments.summary: Detailed design discussion of the RTC area mobility project. Key concerns: no traffic light funding at Carbon/Hiawatha crosswalk (TAP grant restricts to pedestrian/bike only, no vehicular improvements), but flashing beacons may be added for safety. Parks department will be engaged for street trees and canopy coverage. Council members raise snow removal for shared-use paths and green infrastructure design for stormwater management. Shared-use path dimensions vary: 10-foot where right-of-way allows, 5-foot sidewalk in constrained areas. Discussion of bike/pedestrian lane markings and prevention of vehicles parking on paths. Burn Dairy proposed new store at Park/NBT intersection could increase local traffic.

speakers.name: Marino L. Nave
speakers.role: Committee Chair (Public Transportation), Common Councilor District 1

department_refs: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs
processing_notes: TAP (Transportation Alternatives Program) grant funding restricts improvements to pedestrian/bicycle only — no vehicular signal upgrades
Burn Dairy store proposal at Park/NBT — status unknown, would affect project traffic analysis

detected_departments: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs
segments.title: New American Pedestrian Safety Advocacy and Green Infrastructure Design
segments.summary: Councilor Nave shares personal account of witnessing a new American family struggling to walk in the road carrying groceries in winter due to lack of sidewalks, framing the project as essential equity infrastructure. Council member Ehrenreich advocates for green infrastructure integration: snow swales, rain management, and tree canopy to improve year-round usability and reduce maintenance burden. Engineer confirms preliminary design will incorporate these elements. Public comment period closing Friday; public meeting was well-attended. Discussion of regional market expansion plans and proposed Burn Dairy store affecting future traffic patterns.

political_signal.description: Councilor Nave uses personal testimony about new American families to champion pedestrian infrastructure as equity issue; Ehrenreich advocates for green infrastructure integration

speakers.name: Marino L. Nave
speakers.role: Committee Chair (Public Transportation), Common Councilor District 1

department_refs: Department of Parks, Recreation and Youth Programs
processing_notes: Councilor Nave references CNY International Food Fair as key community destination for new American pedestrians

[00:39:28] Park Street.

[00:40:37] head. And for the last 10 years, I've

segments.title: Public Testimony on Park Street Pedestrian Deaths and Sidewalk Infrastructure Gaps
segments.summary: Neighborhood activist John Meyer (Washington Square Neighborhood Watch/Task Force, 5154 Park St) delivers public testimony on pedestrian dangers. Reports 3 deaths in 3 years on Park Street and describes new Americans from nearby mosque walking in roads to reach the regional market. Recounts incident of woman losing groceries when struck by car at unfinished crosswalk. Meyer has published a 10-year goal of walkable access to the regional market. Engineer confirms project will install 2 crosswalks across Park Street, ADA corners with push buttons, and 10-foot shared-use paths connecting to DPW's Washington Square path. Additional questions about bike racks, Veo stations, and bus shelters — funding limited but locations can be designed into plans. Council also raises vehicle parking on shared-use paths as enforcement concern.

political_signal.description: 10-year organized neighborhood advocacy for pedestrian infrastructure, citing pedestrian deaths and dangers to new American community on Park Street north side

speakers.name: John Meyer
speakers.role: Neighborhood Activist, Washington Square Neighborhood Watch

speakers.name: Marino L. Nave
speakers.role: Committee Chair (Public Transportation), Common Councilor District 1

processing_notes: John Meyer reports 3 pedestrian deaths in 3 years on Park Street — significant constituent safety data
Veo bike racks mentioned in context of streetscape amenities (connects to CTX-MANUAL-2026-00001 micromobility record)
Centro (transit) mentioned for bus shelter coordination
